topic: A student discovers their teacher follows them on social media
raw_output:
[story 1]

The notification flashed across my screen, a stark, unwelcome intrusion into the otherwise mundane scrolling of my afternoon. It was Professor Albright. A photo of a well-lit campus quad, captioned with a vague, overly cheerful reflection on 'academic inspiration.' My jaw tightened. I stared at the image, the fluorescent hum of the library suddenly feeling oppressive. I felt a sharp, unpleasant clenching in my chest, a tightening that made my breath shallow. Why? What did he want? It was an invasion, a casual acknowledgment of my existence that felt profoundly misplaced and irritatingly public. I quickly minimized the window, my fingers hovering over the desktop, a restless energy buzzing beneath my skin.

[story 2]

I watched the profile picture load—Professor Albright, looking annoyingly relaxed in what appeared to be a vacation setting. A slow burn started low in my stomach, a simmering heat that made my hands tremble slightly as I gripped my lukewarm coffee mug. Every carefully constructed facade I usually maintained seemed to be crumbling under the weight of this unexpected connection. I reread the caption three times, searching for some hidden meaning, some justification for this digital surveillance. A deep, cold resentment settled over me, heavy and suffocating. I didn't want his approval, or even his notice; I just wanted the silence to return, the clean separation between the classroom and this strange, intrusive online space.

[story 3]

It wasn't even a direct message, just a follow. That was enough. I felt a sudden, sharp recoil, like being brushed by something unclean. I leaned back in my chair, the plastic groaning under my weight, and let out a slow, controlled exhale. My eyes scanned the room, cataloging the other students, searching for anyone else who might have noticed the digital tether. A sour taste coated my tongue. This whole situation felt like a deliberate slight, a demonstration of power I hadn't anticipated. I picked up my textbook, the paper suddenly feeling flimsy and meaningless, and deliberately turned a page, focusing intensely on the dense text, trying to drown out the faint, persistent thrum of annoyance vibrating just beneath my composure.
